comments.text: It is a coincidence by the fact that $20$ was exactly small enough that the sum of reciprocals being equal implied that a short (length 1) sum of reciprocal needed to be involved. If equality would have been true for $n=21$, then there should have been a subset of $[1..20]$ of size $15$ which could be extended by $21.$ Since equal sums of reciprocals do not involve primes between $n/2$ and $n$, these can be neglected in the computation. Then a bruteforce verification (added on the github link) can do the job. For this, note that e.g. $1+\frac 1{21} = \frac1 2 + \frac 13+ \frac 17 + \frac 1{14}$ and $\frac 16 + \frac 1{21} = \frac 17+ \frac{1}{14}$, so there were multiple crucial equalities of (longer) sums of reciprocals involving $21$. On the other hand, $\frac 1a=\frac 1b + \frac1{21}$ has no solution where $0<a<b<21$. The first "most wanted" is caught as such. Here "dead" (false) is less interesting than "alive". (with that idea, it only has been checked that $R(20+x)=14+x$ for $1 \le x \le 3$ without much effort)

Wow, that was fast! That seems like a satisfactory explanation to me. Hopefully we can at least get the sequence R(n) extended enough that it can be sent to the OEIS as a distinct sequence from A384927.
It is extended/computed till $n=39$. At OEIS one usually prefers to also have the code for general $n$, which is not the case here (it would need to be adjusted for larger n).

Followed up on your great work here: https://github.com/teorth/erdosproblems/issues/161#issuecomment-3697977745 I have code for computing all values of N, and extended the sequence up to N=54. All details on GitHub :)

Using linear programming, I also got the following values. $R(21+x)=15+x$ for $0 \le x \le 2$ $R(24+x)=17+x$ for $0 \le x \le 3$ $R(28+x)=20+x$ for $x \in \{0,1\}$, $R(30+x)=21+x$ for $0\le x \le 2$, $R(33+x)=23+x$ for $0\le x \le 1$ and $R(35+x)=24+x$ for $0 \le x \le 4.$ So the sequence starts with $$1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 10, 11, 12, 12, 13, 14, 14, 15, 15,15,\\16,17,17,18,19,20,20,21,21,22,23,23,24,24,25,26,27,28.$$ So for small values, $R(n+1)=R(n)$ if $n+1$ is quite smooth, with some exceptions such as $36.$ (Also, one can have combinations with $p,2p, \ldots, kp$ that do not have $p$ in the denominator for $k$ (polylog) quite small wrt to $p$, is another reason why drawing conclusions is hard) $\textbf{Idea efficient programming}$ The idea was to first find all pairs $(A,B)$ of disjoint subsets of $[n]$ for which the sum of reciprocals is equal. Then $A \cup B$ cannot belong to a maximal set with the desired property. Since $\frac 1{p^k}$ cannot be equal to $\sum_B 1/b$ when $v_p(b)<k$ for every $b \in B$, and $\frac 1p \pm \frac 2p$ also has a factor $p$ in the denominator, we know that $p,2p$ for $p> \frac n3$ and $16,32,27,25$ never belong to such sets $A \cup B.$ This made the computation of forbidden sets $A \cup B$ more efficient. (for $n=32,$ there were already $81949$ many of them, for $n=33$ there were $245435$, for $n=35$ around $700k$ and for $n=36$ there were $2302966$, indicating that it becomes hard to compute; 2 hours with a common pc for $n=36$) The program has been added for $n=36$ in https://github.com/teorth/erdosproblems/issues/161.

Initial numerical computation suggests that $R(N)$ agrees with OEIS A384927 which is the maximum size of a subset $S \subseteq \{1 \cdots N\}$ such that for distinct $a,b \in S$, $(a + b)$ does not divide $ab$ - however, it is not immediately clear to me how these conditions are related as they do not seem to be equivalent.
We have $\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b} = \frac{a+b}{ab}$. So if $a+b$ does divide $ab$, then $\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}$ is a unit fraction as well. That at least shows that these conditions are related.
I should have specified - the relation for 2 fractions is clear to me, but the generalization from just 2 to more than 2 is what I am stuck on.
This seems like a good use case for the "help wanted" tag at the Erdos problems database. I've sent out the "bat-signal", let's see if anyone offers to help.
